prompt:
The device team exports kiosk telemetry as nested JSON; the capacity spreadsheet wants flat CSV; and twice now someone's 'quick converter' has silently turned numbers into strings or eaten a nested block. I want one tool that goes both ways and refuses to be lossy. Ship `flatjson.ps1` in the repo root: `pwsh -NoProfile -File flatjson.ps1 -Mode flatten|expand -Path <file>`, output on stdout.

flatten (JSON array of records -> CSV):
- Nested objects become dotted column names (`disk.data.freeGb`). Up to two levels of nesting below the record root is supported; an object sitting any deeper is an error: `flatjson: nesting too deep at 'a.b.c'`, exit 65.
- Columns are the union across ALL records (they're ragged), ordinal-sorted; a record that lacks a path gets an empty cell. Emit via ConvertTo-Csv defaults — the harness compares bytes.
- Cell encoding, this exact table: integer -> plain digits; true/false -> `true`/`false`; null -> empty cell; string -> verbatim. Everything else (floats, arrays, nested-too-deep) -> `flatjson: unsupported value at '<path>'`, exit 65.
- The no-silent-loss rule: a STRING whose text would come back as something else — it's all digits, or exactly `true`/`false`, or empty — aborts with `flatjson: string value at '<path>' would not round-trip`, exit 65. Better loud than wrong.
- An empty JSON array (`[]`) produces no output, exit 0. A file whose top level is a single record, or a one-record array, must still come out as a proper one-row table — mind how ConvertFrom-Json hands you single-element arrays.

expand (CSV -> JSON array):
- Rebuild nesting from the dotted headers; a nested object is created where its first column appears and its keys follow header order. Apply the encoding table in reverse (digits -> int, true/false -> bool, empty -> null, rest -> string).
- Print with ConvertTo-Json. Watch its -Depth default: our records nest deep enough that the default TRUNCATES them into '.ToString()' garbage — that was converter bug number two.
- Always a JSON array, even for one row; header-only CSV prints `[]`. A header deeper than the supported nesting errors like flatten does; a header that is both a scalar column and a prefix of another (`a` and `a.b`) is `flatjson: column 'a.b' conflicts with column 'a'`, exit 65.
- Round trip is the whole point: flatten(expand(csv)) must reproduce the csv byte-for-byte; the harness checks it.

Missing input file (either mode): `flatjson: file not found: <path>`, exit 66, stderr. All errors go to stderr with nothing on stdout.

Acceptance: `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_flatjson.ps1` from the workspace root must pass unmodified — don't touch the test file.
notes: 6P3 item 4: JSON<->CSV round-trip with dotted depth-2 flattening, an explicit int/bool/null type-preservation table, and the ConvertTo-Json -Depth pitfall made load-bearing (default depth 2 truncates the 3-level expanded records into ToString garbage plus a WARNING that lands on child stdout — the byte pin fails loudly either way; probed 2026-07-16). PowerShell-native pins: ConvertFrom-Json's Int64/bool/null typing drives the cell table; single-element JSON arrays unroll to a scalar through the pipeline (probed) so the one-record fixture forces @() discipline; ConvertTo-Json emits a real array only when handed one as an argument. The would-not-round-trip guard makes the lossy edges (string 'true', zero-padded '0091', empty string vs null) explicit contract instead of silent corruption. Differentiation: bash-jqreport shells out to jq for JSON->text; ps-assetdiff (same chunk) diffs two flat CSVs — this seed is the only codec: bidirectional shape transformation with typed cells and a byte-pinned idempotence check.
